text: Narrative 1 - 'I was training on the X Scope and traffic volume was fairly high compared to the previous several weeks.  ZZZ was landing Runway X and ZZZ1 Runway Y Right; making for a complex approach pattern.  Aircraft Y was NW bound toward ZZZZZ; the South fix for RNAV X approach at ZZZ level at 030.  Aircraft X was on the Localizer Y Right approach at ZZZ1 east of ZZZZZ1; descending out of 030 for 020; not cleared for the approach.  The close proximity was recognized as Aircraft X (now at 025; still descending) was established on the localizer and Aircraft Y (still level at 030) was approximately 1 NM away from ZZZZZ; the situation was recognized just as 3 mile separation was lost.  I turned Aircraft X to a 180 HDG as they were out of 022 for 020 to avoid a further loss of separation.Aircraft X was slow to descend and I did not recognize the altitude in sufficient time.  Training has been intermittent for a long time due to the COVID situation.  Also; unusually frequent storms and bad weather had kept traffic volume low for the past few weeks; keeping training complexity low as well.  Today presented lots of training and volume; I was getting tired due to not being conditioned for constant traffic and lost focus.  I would [recommend that I] recognize my own fatigue and ask for training or to work a slower or less complex position for a mid day break or to change things up.'
rationale: The trainee controller was operating during an unusually high-traffic day after a prolonged period of low-complexity training due to COVID-related schedule disruptions, and by his own account was growing fatigued and losing focus from being unconditioned to sustained high-volume operations. This fatigue and degraded attentional capacity caused him to miss Aircraft X's slow descent rate until separation had already been lost. The approach separation procedures were correct and in place; the root cause was the human operator's physiological and cognitive state—fatigue from reconditioning demands—rather than any deficiency in the documented procedures.

text: Narrative 1 - 'As of the time the aircraft door closed we had not been catered.  I restocked the carts with the supplies we had. The boarding door was closed and all doors had been armed using proper procedures for monitor and challenge.  We were waiting to be deiced. I left my door to retrieve my demo equipment (In the last overhead bin aircraft Left.) I heard a knock at the door and saw the caterer looking through the window.  I crossed my wrists to motion that the door WAS NOT safe to open. The door handle began to rise and I grabbed the handle pushing it to the closed position; hoping to signal him to discontinue opening the door.  He continued to open the door and I was screaming and banging on the door. (The AFA told me she could hear me in the front of the aircraft!) He discontinued opening the door and the inside handle was at a half-way position. I called the flight deck to advise them of the situation and I was told not to touch the door.  They would call maintenance.  The catering truck pulled away from the aircraft.After several minutes; I went to the flight deck to communicate with the Captain the details of this incident. I was at the doorway to the flight deck when the dreaded 'hissing' of the slide could be heard throughout the airplane. I returned to my door to find the catering truck parked at the 2L door and the ripped remains of the slide caught on the ramp of the truck.'
rationale: After all aircraft doors had been armed, a caterer arrived to deliver catering and forced the door open despite the flight attendant's clear visual refusal signals—crossed wrists and physically holding the door handle—causing the armed emergency slide to deploy. The incident reveals an absence of a reliable procedure to prevent ground service personnel from accessing aircraft with armed doors: no interphone contact was required, no door-armed indicator was visible to the caterer on the exterior, and no formal coordination protocol existed between the cabin crew and ground services before catering access was permitted post-arming. Because a correctly-followed procedure from the caterer's side would still have resulted in the same door-open action—the information that the door was armed was not formally communicated to them—this is a procedure error in the ground service coordination workflow.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'I'm a student pilot. This was my first solo; and I was going to do a touch and go. I executed the traffic pattern and then on final I was a high and trying to descend while also trying to maintain the center line of the runway. I proceeded to land and the aircraft bounced twice which led to the loss of the nose wheel tire. I exited the runway immediately and contacted Ground Control. Prior to this in the earlier hours I had done 6 takeoffs and landings with my instructor; who then proceeded to leave me for solo.'
Narrative 2 - 'I am Person A; the instructor of student Person B. I have been guiding my student's training from the beginning. Since the first flight; she has always shown a bit of nervousness when making decisions in the aircraft. She is a very diligent and studious student. During the last few flights before Stage Check 1; she was having difficulties with her traffic pattern; and her major issue was maintaining a stable; safe final approach and keeping her center line. She would always get a little nervous during the final phase of the flight in the landing flare. When she was somewhat ready for solo after completing the basic requirements; I decided to send her for the Stage Check. She passed the ground part but failed the flight. Afterward; we had some ground sessions to explain how she could improve; and even after two more flights; I noticed that the student still needed a bit more. So; I sent her for a flight with a senior instructor to assess her progress. Today; during the flight with the senior instructor; my student performed well throughout the flight; including the traffic pattern; approach; and landings. The senior instructor decided to endorse her for her first solo as she was showing good results and good landings. When she took off and completed the traffic pattern; she prepared to land. Just as she was about to touch down; she tried to correct as she was a bit high; so she lowered the nose to adjust; resulting in a bounced landing.Consequently; she landed forcefully on the nose wheel; causing a flat tire on the nose and a prop strike. She proceeded to exit the runway and report the incident to the school. The school came to pick her up from the spot where she was and to retrieve the aircraft.'
rationale: The student pilot had a documented history of instability on final approach and anxiety during the landing flare, and two flights prior to solo she had still not fully resolved these deficiencies per her primary instructor's assessment. Despite this, a senior instructor who observed only a single satisfactory session endorsed her for first solo, and she then reverted to her characteristic unstable final on the very first solo attempt, resulting in a bounced landing, nose-wheel tire failure, and prop strike. The correct landing procedures were known to the student; the incident stems from a premature qualification decision—a human factors failure of training judgment—where the endorsing instructor's assessment did not adequately account for the student's persistent pattern of instability.

text: Narrative 1 - 'After takeoff on the WYLSN5 out of HOU; we were switched to Houston departure. Checked in leaving 3800 feet for 16;000 feet. ATC queried us on the altitude cleared to; we said 16;000 feet. ATC returned and said we were only cleared to 5000 feet. After being told we were cleared to 5000 ft; I set 5000 ft in the alerter leaving approximately 4100 for 5000 feet. We leveled off at 5000 ft. ATC stated that direct to 16;000 feet clearances are never given; usually stop at five. He asked if 5000 was in our Clearance; we stated we received it PDC and only saw the 16;000 ft clearance limit of the departure. He said they have been having problems with PDC clearances out of Hobby with this same exact instance with pilots not seeing the 5000 ft limit. He said he would make note of this and review the PDC. We continued the flight and approximately 15 minutes later was asked to call an 800 number when we landed for a possible navigation deviation. We called after landing and explained the situation we were told that no deviation occurred; but the PDC was apparently incorrectly copied.We were rushed and sidetracked as a crew in HOU with a passenger trip; and both apparently did not see the except maintain 5000 ft in the notes at the end of the PDC after both confirming 16000 ft. We only saw the 16000 ft altitude given as the top of the departure. As a crew we need to slow down and make sure every word has been read on the PDC and copied and entered correctly. It would also be beneficial if ATC reformatted the PDC clearances and standardized them including any changes to a departure altitude towards the top of the clearance where the departure is given so it can be understood easily.'
rationale: The PDC clearance placed the critical altitude restriction—'except maintain 5,000 ft'—in a notes section at the end of the document, separated from the departure altitude of 16,000 ft listed earlier, and ATC confirmed this exact formatting issue had caused the same misread by multiple crews out of Hobby Airport. Both pilots independently read back 16,000 ft without noticing the exception, consistent with the predictable scanning behavior of crews whose attention is drawn to the primary altitude field. The procedure error lies in the PDC format design: placing a binding altitude restriction in a non-prominent trailing notes field, rather than adjacent to the departure altitude it modifies, created a systematic document design flaw that repeatedly caused crews to miss a safety-critical clearance element.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X departed SLC on a 340 heading climbing to 100. I acknowledged his check on but did not climb him out of 100. His routing was a little strange; so I asked my hand-off to coordinate direct RKS and 150; while I checked the ASD-X to ensure that there was not conflicting traffic behind him and that the coordinated altitude would work. In the meantime I had another controlled come sit down next to me and said they were going to take the position; and several other aircraft checked on. When I returned to Aircraft X to give the new routing and altitude; I saw that he was quickly climbing out of 102; and I mistakenly assumed that I had already given him the 150 altitude; so I did not also include that in the instruction. Aircraft X quickly continued the climb to 106; and I started giving the briefing. As I began the briefing I saw that Aircraft X was descending again; and I instructed them to expedite the climb to 150 and turned them away from terrain; but they did not respond. I issued a low altitude alert and instructed them that they were about to enter a higher MVA and advised them to expedite the climb through 120. This time they read back the instruction and started the climb. They entered an 110 MVA at 102; and I again issued the heading away from higher terrain; which they did read back. The aircraft quickly climbed out of the MVA and was turned back on course. I need to make sure to reissue instructions when I'm not 100% certain they received them; just to be on the safe side.'
rationale: During a position relief briefing—while simultaneously managing active aircraft—the controller issued a coordination request for a new routing and altitude for Aircraft X but was then distracted by the incoming controller sitting down and multiple new check-ons, causing him to forget he had not yet transmitted the altitude clearance to the aircraft. When he observed Aircraft X climbing, he incorrectly assumed the clearance had already been given and did not re-issue it, allowing the aircraft to enter a higher minimum vectoring altitude environment without authorization. The procedural failure lies in the position relief process itself: conducting an active briefing handoff while the outgoing controller retained responsibility for live traffic created a foreseeable distraction vector that caused a critical instruction to be omitted and then misremembered as having been given.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was assigned to Aircraft X. I went to get a tool box but it was already taken out. We loaded the flight. I held the freight Hazmat box because we did not have 15 bags. The mail did not show up. I was trying to lock the flight out to get it pushed out on time. I remarked the Hazmat as OTH; but I forgot to remark the mail as NSH. These kind of mistakes can happen when you have to load a flight in less 15 minutes.'
rationale: Facing a load-out window of under 15 minutes—well below normal—the ramp agent correctly remarked the Hazmat items but omitted the corresponding NSH remark for the undelivered mail before closing the flight, producing a weight discrepancy. The agent explicitly identified the root cause as the time compression of the abbreviated load window. The documentation procedures for weight-and-balance close-out were correct; the human operator's prospective memory failed under time pressure, causing an incomplete close-out sequence—a classic human factors error driven by urgency rather than any ambiguity in the required procedure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'While passing over TONIE there appeared to be a square-ish black and grey drone that passed below and on the left side of our aircraft. Approx 300-500 below us. Did not notice it until it was passing by. No evasive maneuver conducted.'
rationale: A drone passed approximately 300–500 feet below the aircraft near TONIE, creating an airspace intrusion by an operator who was either unaware of or disregarding the airspace constraints applicable to UAS operations at that altitude and location. The flight crew had no warning prior to the drone appearing and no time to take evasive action. This is a human factors incident driven by an external operator's failure of situational awareness and regulatory compliance, not by any deficiency in ATC procedures or crew performance—the crew acted appropriately given zero reaction time.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'My co-worker was performing work with the LH IB fan cowl open and I stated that I need to transfer some hydraulics then I was going to perform a hydraulic standby pump operation test. The test calls for the flaps to be set at 1. I started to perform the test without him in front to clear and the flap contacted the IB cowl on the LH engine.Suggestion - Pay attention to procedures and read the AMM carefully. Take time and be aware of your surroundings.'
Narrative 2 - 'While I was finishing up a moisture removal on LH Eng; I had a discussion with my co-worker about what was needed to finish up on the aircraft for the evening. I needed to finish the Moisture removal and do Test 32; Engine Idle System Test. He needs to finish a Standby Hydraulic Actuation System Test. I still had Inbd fan Cowl open while I was finishing up the removal of tools to prepare for Test 32. He went up into aircraft while I was finishing up. Next thing I heard was the hydraulic pumps coming on and before I could get up to find out what was going on the LH Inbd Kruger Flap made momentary contact with the LH inbd Fan Cowl. We had a miscommunication in that he started to do his Test before I finished my maintenance. I also missed a step to lock out the flaps after I deactivated the hydraulics in my haste to accomplish my assigned task.Suggestion - A More Detailed card that doesn't require one to go to several other references to complete an action. Take more time to more carefully review all references for all maintenance actions.'
rationale: One maintenance technician began a hydraulic standby pump operation test—requiring the flaps to be set to position 1—before his coworker had finished working on and was clear of the left inboard fan cowl, causing the extending Kruger flap to contact the open cowl. The second technician also acknowledged missing the AMM step to lock out the flaps after deactivating hydraulics, compounding the hazard. Both failures trace to a communication breakdown between the two technicians about task sequencing and incomplete adherence to the AMM lockout/clearance procedure—human factors of coordination and procedural compliance rather than any deficiency in the written maintenance instructions.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Airport Ops called me on the land line and asked me if I had spoken to an Aircraft X. I did not recall speaking to an aircraft with that callsign; but I told him I'd check the tapes to verify. He informed me that he found the aircraft on the north ramp and believed that he had landed and taxied without any ATC authorization. According to airport ops there were 3 souls on the aircraft; 2 of which were deaf and mute and the third had no identification and he claimed that he was given ATC authorization to land.Upon further investigation we established that the aircraft entered the Class Delta airspace and landed without ever speaking to ATC. I did not see the aircraft since it was dark and he landed on the east runway which can be difficult to see aircraft during the day when you're looking for them.There's a lot you could do in this situation I think it was just a fluke occurrence.'
rationale: An aircraft entered a Class D airport, landed, and taxied to the ramp without establishing two-way radio contact with ATC at any point—a mandatory requirement for Class D airspace entry—and went undetected because it was dark and the east runway was difficult to observe visually. The Class D airspace procedures are entirely dependent on pilot-initiated radio contact and ATC visual surveillance, with no procedural safeguard—such as radar detection, transponder requirement enforcement, or physical access control—to identify or intercept an aircraft that simply bypasses the communication requirement. Because a compliant procedure by ATC was in place but structurally unable to detect or prevent non-compliant entry, the failure is systemic and procedural rather than an error of individual controller performance.

text: Narrative 1 - 'When I examined the rudder rod ends to replace them with upgraded with grease fitting rod ends per Engineering Authorization (EA); I found that the rod ends that were installed previously on aircraft X were not safety wired. I initiated non routine [check] to make sure that rod ends were safety wired before the aircraft left the hangar. I wanted to ensure that the aircraft would be safe in flight. I am concerned that there might be other aircraft in our fleet or in other carrier's fleets that are also flying without the rudder rod ends being safety wired. I highly recommend that an investigation to discover where the work on the rudder was performed is initiated and then that all aircraft that were worked at that location or that person be examined to ensure that the rudder is in a safe condition for flight.I would also like to ask that the individual that performed the work; signed for the work; and inspected the work be informed that the safety wire was not installed to make sure that they know it should be to prevent this from occurring in the future again.Suggested Resolution: 'I am concerned that there might be other aircraft in our fleet or in other carrier's fleets that are also flying without the rudder rod ends being safety wired. I highly recommend that an investigation to discover where the work on the rudder was performed is initiated and then that all aircraft that were worked at that location or that person be examined to ensure that the rudder is in a safe condition for flight. I would also like to ask that the individual that performed the work; signed for the work; and inspected the work be informed that the safety wire was not installed to make sure that they know it should be to prevent this from occurring in the future again.''
rationale: Rudder rod ends were installed on the aircraft without safety wire, and the work was both signed off by the performing technician and accepted by an inspector without the missing safety wire being detected. The maintenance quality control process—specifically the dual-layer check of technician self-certification and independent inspection—failed to catch a critical safety item that should be visually obvious during any review. Because two separate verification steps both failed to surface the omission, the root cause is a procedural one: the inspection and sign-off protocol was insufficient to ensure that a safety-critical installation requirement was verified independently before return to service.

text:
Narrative 1 - 'On take-off runway XX; received flashing green hydraulic pump low pressure. Landing gear would not retract. The ECAM was indicating that the green pumps failed. Leveled at 2000 ft. [Requested priority handling] with ZZZ departure. After 2-3 minutes; the hydraulic issue stopped. Pumps back to normal. Raised landing gear. No other issues noted. I continued to ZZZ1 for priority handling. No issues with configuring for landing.Aircraft was released from contract heavy maintenance for a right main landing gear change. Maintenance suspects air in green hydraulic system.Possible issue with heavy maintenance at ZZZ.'
Narrative 2 - 'On departure from RWY XX; when landing gear was selected 'Up'; the gear did not retract and a hydraulic warning was indicated on the Airbus ECAM notification system. The Captain (Pilot Monitoring) [requested priority handling] with ATC and we entered a radar box pattern at 2;000 ft. to stay within appropriate distance of ZZZ runways. The Pilot Monitoring began to accomplish the ECAM steps. Within 2-3 minutes; the hydraulic warning indication recovered and the landing gear was able to be retracted normally. The Captain recovered to ZZZ1 with priority. The aircraft was able to be configured normally for an uneventful landing. Maintenance had recently been accomplished on the right Main Landing Gear. Unknown to what extent that maintenance may have contributed to our situation; but maintenance at ZZZ1 suspected air in the hydraulic line resulting from heavy maintenance. Possible issue with heavy maintenance on right main landing gear at ZZZ.'
rationale: The aircraft was released from heavy contract maintenance following a right main landing gear change, but was returned to service with air in the green hydraulic system—a defect that manifested immediately on the first gear retraction after departure, causing hydraulic pressure failure and requiring priority handling. Maintenance at the service center suspected the air intrusion resulted from the landing gear work itself. The return-to-service procedure after major landing gear maintenance failed to include or adequately verify hydraulic system bleeding and integrity, allowing a direct consequence of the maintenance action to reach the operational flight crew undetected.

text: Narrative 1 - 'DFW was in a south flow departing runways 18L and 17C. Due to weather and ZFW route restrictions; aircraft departing Runway 18L were on vectors and the Runway 17C departures were still on the RNAV routing. DR1; DR2; and DR3 sectors were all open. I was conducting On the Job Training (OJT) on DR2.An Airbus; departed on the FORCK SID and was talking with DR1. Approximately 3 miles in trail a CRJ2; departed on the NELYN SID talking to DR2. Both aircraft came off of Runway 17C. While my trainee and I were discussing how conventional departures off of DAL were drifting into the downwind; we noticed the overtake speeds of the CRJ-200 indicating 240 knots while Airbus was indicating 180 knots.A loss of separation had already occurred. We stopped the CRJ-200 at 5000 feet and turned them to a 200 heading to reestablish separation. We were also coordinating with DR3 verbally as we were turning into traffic they had off of Runway 18L that was on the 190 heading. We had enough lateral separation with DR3 that we were not concerned with our 200 heading. I asked the CRJ-200 if DFW tower had provided visual separation on departure to see if the coordination was just not passed. THE CRJ-200 said no Visual Separation was given; but they did have traffic in sight. Once separation was reestablished; we turned and climbed the CRJ-200 back on course.I wrote up an ATSAP report about a week or two ago with concerns about this very problem. The RNAV procedures have assigned speeds of 'at or below 240kts.' Two aircraft that are on a similar flight path are given 3 miles in trail. The front aircraft decides to go slow; while the trailing aircraft decides to increase to 240 knots. This creates an immediate overtake situation where altitude separation cannot always be ensured. About 10 minutes prior to this situation; DR1 had two MRSSH departures that ended up 1 mile in trail. DR1 had to work extra hard to provide ZFW the appropriate 5 mile constant or increasing separation according to our LOA. DR1 was able to maintain altitude separation the entire time. While the RNAV departures work in theory; the speeds need to be changed where the pilots do not have discretion to go any speed they want while a trailing aircraft is overtaking them.'
rationale: The RNAV departure procedures at DFW assigned aircraft on similar flight paths only 3 miles in trail while permitting speed discretion up to 240 knots, a combination that predictably and repeatedly produced overtake situations where trailing aircraft closed on lead aircraft before altitude separation could be guaranteed. The reporter had filed an ATSAP report about the identical structural problem weeks earlier, and a nearly identical event occurred on the same sector within 10 minutes of this incident. The procedure's inherent design flaw—unconstrained speed discretion paired with minimum trail spacing on converging flight paths—was the root cause, not any controller error; this is a documented, recurring procedure failure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Upon arrival; I noticed that there [were] no agents available. After waiting a while I contacted dispatch and we confirmed the min fuel for the flight was 2;600lbs (2;582 lbs on the release). The ramp said that we could have a takeoff weight of 3;050 lbs based on our assumed fuel burn and landing weight at our destination. Since there was no [gate] agent I called Dispatch and told them to contact the fueler and tell them to fuel to 3;300 lbs; this includes 250 lbs for taxi since there were delays and it was the only runway we could use due to the winds at. We departed after a longer than usual taxi waiting for traffic. The flight went normally until I noticed that our fuel gauges seemed to be reading the same amount as when we confirmed our fuel load prior to engine start. We looked for a check list but there was none so we contacted dispatch and confirmed our fuel load and requested to talk with maintenance while they confirmed our fuel order with International Fuel. I discussed the issue with maintenance and it seemed like the gauges were actually fine. That meant only one thing could have happened; we had been overfueled by about 1;000 lbs. Dispatch confirmed that the fuel vendor said they fueled the aircraft to 4;300 lbs; not 3;300 lbs. We asked Dispatch if they wanted us to return or burn off fuel and land at our destination. They said that [dispatch] wanted us to continue. We descended to 4;000 feet to hold and burn fuel faster over the VOR. We talked to the Chief Pilot and he agreed that we should land at our destination. We burned fuel until we were at our planned landing weight and landed without incident. A maintenance inspection was performed the next morning and no defects were found due to the overweight operation.'
rationale: The fueling order of 3,300 lbs was communicated from the crew through dispatch to the fuel vendor, with no gate agent present to directly coordinate or verify the transaction, and the vendor fueled to 4,300 lbs without any confirmation back to the crew. The overfuel of approximately 1,000 lbs was not discovered until the crew noticed fuel gauges reading unexpectedly high in flight. The fuel ordering and verification procedure lacked any direct crew-to-fueler confirmation step or pre-departure quantity cross-check that would have caught the discrepancy on the ground, making this a procedure gap rather than a flight crew performance failure.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We didn't receive the dangerous goods summary until we had started the pushback. I sent it via ACARS once the pushback was complete.'
rationale: The dangerous goods summary—a required pre-departure document—was not provided to the crew until after pushback had already commenced, meaning the aircraft began moving before the crew had reviewed or acknowledged the DG load information. The crew transmitted the summary via ACARS only after pushback was complete, normalizing a late-documentation sequence that violates the intent of the DG pre-departure requirement. The workflow procedure governing DG summary delivery to the flight deck failed to ensure the document reached the crew within the required pre-departure window, representing a systemic gap in the DG documentation process rather than a crew oversight.

text: Narrative 1 - 'The First Officer was Pilot Flying; and touched down at the 1000 foot marker on Runway XXL. A second or two after touchdown; around 120 knots; the Tower Controller called us; instructing us to exit at Taxiway X; with traffic on short final. This was a critical phase of flight; and I was concentrating on thrust reverser and spoiler deployment; so I ignored the Controller. I sensed that the FO felt rushed to immediately transfer controls so that he could reply to the Controller. I instructed the FO to continue (meaning with control of the aircraft); and I remarked to the FO somewhat angrily (about the Controller; not the FO) to ignore the Controller. Still; I did not reply to the Controller. At that moment; maybe around 90 or 100 knots; the Controller again told us to exit Taxiway X; because of traffic on short final that 'would have to go-around;' in the Controller's words. At this point; I was extremely annoyed; and I tried my best to relay to the FO to continue decelerating and that we would take whatever exit we felt was safe. I believe the Controller called us around 60 or 70 knots again; telling us to exit; and I transferred controls from the FO to me. I began to locate Taxiway X and planned further deceleration and exit from the runway. The FO replied to the Controller; acknowledging our exit. I then got on the radio as I was exiting the runway and told the Controller something like; 'You called us at 120 knots;' implying to the Controller that he had done an extremely unprofessional job of managing traffic and interrupting us during a critical phase of flight. For the remaining taxi in; my adrenaline was high; and I was having trouble focusing on our taxi routing and instruction. I also forgot to turn off the seat belt sign at the gate; and the Flight Attendants had to call me to do so.This was a completely unnecessary and distracting action on behalf of the Tower Controller. I am amazed that Controllers are taught not to distract Pilots during critical phases of flight; yet this Controller chose to do so. I'd like to ask the Controller if he'd prefer causing a mishap on the runway due to his distracting actions; versus making the traffic on final execute a go-around. I have seen this type of action by Controllers more and more lately. For the first time in my years of professional flying; I am questioning the widespread quality and training of Controllers.'
rationale: The Tower Controller transmitted to the landing crew three times during the high-speed rollout—beginning at approximately 120 knots—demanding they exit at a specific taxiway due to traffic on short final, directly interrupting a critical phase of flight in a manner that distracted the Captain, elevated crew stress, and degraded cockpit coordination for the remainder of the ground roll and taxi. The established ATC operating standard discourages non-safety-critical transmissions to landing aircraft during rollout precisely because of this risk; the controller's repeated calls prioritized expediting the following traffic over sterile-cockpit discipline. This is a procedure error: the controller's actions violated the operational norm protecting crews from avoidable cockpit distraction during landing rollout.

text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X was on the new METROPLEX RNAV approach inbound to LGB. The pilot was given the phraseology to descend via except maintain 5000. The pilot read back everything correctly but was confused by what that meant with the new procedures. The pilot immediately started to descend to 5000 instead of complying with the crossing restrictions of the arrival. Since it's a new procedure; I asked the pilot if he was descending with arrival which the pilot responded yes. When I pulled up the map he was way below the arrival and also below the MVA.It's early in the Metroplex implementation but some of the pilots still seem confused on what they are supposed to be doing with the procedure changes.'
rationale: A new METROPLEX RNAV procedure introduced 'descend via except maintain' phraseology, but at least one pilot immediately misapplied it—descending directly to the stated altitude rather than following the arrival's crossing restrictions—and ended up well below the arrival profile and the minimum vectoring altitude. The controller noted this was early in the Metroplex implementation and that pilot confusion with the new procedures was ongoing. The procedure error lies in the implementation: the new clearance phraseology was placed into operational use without ensuring that affected pilots had received adequate training to correctly interpret and execute it, making pilot misapplication a foreseeable and systemic consequence of the rollout.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was holding short of Runway 4 at E63 (Gila Bend Municipal) waiting to takeoff eastbound back to my home airport; ZZZ. Listening to the CTAF while on the ground; I hear an aircraft announce their position in the left downwind for Runway 4. I do not recall hearing a position call from the other aircraft involved on what runway he was intending to land on. I knew he was coming from the north-northwest but you would think he would join the pattern for Runway 4 with everyone else. I didn't have any expectation bias because I checked left and right while holding short before announcing my call to take off on Runway 4. I knew there was one aircraft in the downwind for 4 and I hadn't heard a call for any other runway so I called to take off on Runway 4 when I deemed it clear. I taxied onto the runway. Once on centerline; I put in full power. Not expecting to see an aircraft on short final for the opposite runway; I hear the other aircraft make the call saying he is coming straight in for Runway 22. I look up and immediately see what looked like a Cessna; coming in to land. I only got a quick glance but I am pretty sure it was a Cessna. I immediately pulled my power to idle and used my brakes to get off on the soonest available runway possible. I still had plenty of time to react so I did not feel super pressured in doing so. I made the right decision getting off the runway as soon as possible and made a call saying I was aborting my takeoff. The other aircraft then realized we were using Runway 4 and not 22 and said that he was now making a right turn to enter the left traffic downwind for Runway 4 after I made my call and got off the runway. I ran my appropriate checklists and taxied back to the approach end of Runway 4 to takeoff again and had enough time to do so before the aircraft made it onto his base and final legs. I announced my action to take off on Runway 4 and did so after making sure I was one hundred percent clear to. I looked left and right and listened to the position calls. When I took off I headed eastbound toward ZZZ.'
rationale: The conflicting aircraft joined straight-in for Runway 22 without making sufficient position calls to alert other traffic in the pattern that they were using the opposite runway direction, causing the reporting pilot—who had correctly listened on CTAF, checked visually, and heard only a single downwind announcement for Runway 4—to reasonably conclude the pattern was clear before announcing his own takeoff. When the straight-in aircraft finally announced its position, the reporting pilot was already rolling on Runway 4. The situational awareness failure belongs to the conflicting pilot, who did not communicate adequate position information in the non-towered environment to allow other traffic to anticipate an opposing runway use.

text: Narrative 1 - 'During arrival at the COS airport; I received clearance for a downwind leg runway 35L and I began descending down to pattern altitude of 7100' msl. It was night and difficult to see the ground; however; I realized during descent that I was getting too close to the ground over a congested area. I am immediately began a climb up to a higher altitude until I was closer to the airport. I realized that the area to the north of Colorado Springs Airport is significantly higher in elevation than the airport itself. In the future; I will be maintaining a higher altitude until closer to the airport.'
rationale: At night, the pilot accepted a clearance for a downwind leg to Runway 35L and descended toward pattern altitude without accounting for the significantly elevated terrain north of Colorado Springs, which is substantially higher than the airport field elevation, resulting in a closer-than-intended proximity to the ground over a congested area. The pilot self-identified the lack of terrain awareness as the root cause and committed to maintaining a higher altitude until closer to the airport on future arrivals. The airspace and approach procedures were functioning correctly; the incident was a human situational awareness failure—specifically terrain awareness—exacerbated by nighttime visual conditions that removed the normal visual terrain cues.

text: Narrative 1 - 'I was working Ground Control and CIC combined during this incident. The only other Controller in the Tower was working Local Control. Aircraft X; a helicopter; was on an RNAV approach and landing. When Aircraft X was 1/2 mile final to the left runway the Local Controller cleared Aircraft Y; a C172 Skyhawk; for takeoff from the right runway. The distance between the center-lines is approximately 320 ft. No traffic was given between the two aircraft. No caution wake turbulence/rotor wash from the landing helicopter.Aircraft X hovered down the right runway and then made a right turnout just past midfield to hover-taxi to land at the helipads. They hovered at about 50 feet. Meanwhile; Aircraft Y had taken the right runway for departure and began his departure roll. Still no traffic calls were exchanged between the aircraft.I asked the Local Controller 'Are you going to say anything?' and pointed at the helicopter. 'Whatever!' the other Controller replied. 'They won't hit.' 'That is [expletive deleted] dangerous!' I said as Aircraft X was halfway across the grass field between the left runway and the helipads. Hovering at about 50 feet AGL when Aircraft Y passed directly over Aircraft X about 200 to 300 feet. The other Controller was not phased in the least about the situation. No altitude restrictions were given; no separation was ensured even when I brought it to the other Controller's attention. It was pure luck that the Aircraft Y's climb rate kept it above Aircraft X. I believe that if there were a member of Management in the Tower; things would have been completely different. The Local Controller would have actually done their job instead of being lazy and not ensuring any kind of separation. This is not the first time situations like this have occurred with this Controller in my presence; but this is definitely the most dangerous one!'
rationale: The Local Controller cleared a Cessna 172 for departure from the right runway while a helicopter was on a half-mile final to the parallel left runway—320 feet of centerline separation—issuing no traffic advisories, no wake turbulence or rotor wash caution, and no separation assurance, and then dismissed a direct warning from the Ground Controller with 'Whatever! They won't hit.' The helicopter subsequently hovered at 50 feet AGL as the departing C172 passed 200–300 feet overhead. While the individual controller's attitude reflects a human failure, the broader classification is procedure: the facility had no active supervisory oversight mechanism capable of intervening in real time when a controller was observed deliberately bypassing mandatory separation procedures, and the pattern of similar incidents with this controller indicates a systemic failure of the facility's safety management and accountability process.

text: Narrative 1 - 'We were cleared to land on Runway XX and tower advised up to keep it rolling down to the taxiway. Shortly after bringing the reversers to idle; we saw Aircraft X just at the top of our windshield fly above and ahead of us landing on Runway XY. We estimated it was roughly 80 to100 ft above and slightly ahead of us as we were rolling down Runway XX.'
rationale: The crew was cleared to land on Runway XX and instructed to keep rolling toward the taxiway, then observed another aircraft land on intersecting Runway XY approximately 80–100 feet above and slightly ahead of them during rollout—indicating that simultaneous landing clearances were issued for intersecting runways without adequate separation assurance. The Tower's procedure for managing simultaneous or closely sequenced operations on intersecting runways failed to prevent two aircraft from occupying converging runway surfaces within a critically close vertical margin. This is a procedure error because the ATC runway management process—not any crew action—created the intersection conflict by issuing or permitting clearances that placed two aircraft on crossing runways simultaneously.
